title: What is the rationale for CaseRule::compare being a vector of SigSpec and not a single Constant?
body:
From reading the manual, it was my understanding that CaseRule is similar to the C case statement:

> Such an object is very similar to the C switch statement as it uses a control signal (\reset in this case) to determine which of its cases should be active.

But then I have read the code and was confused, because it does not look like the C case statement at all! Indeed, CaseRule has several compares and each compare may be a dynamic signal. So something like the following is valid RTLIL:

```
switch { a b }
  case c, d
    ...
end
```

I do not understand why this is the case (no pun intended). Doesn't this complicate RTLIL while adding no expressive power?

I also understand that the cells generated by `proc_mux` for `case c, d` would be different than cells generated for equivalent `case {c d}`, and I can see how this can affect further optimization. Is there a specific purpose for this?

Edit: I've looked closer and it looks like Verilog allows this kind of case statement. Is this just for roundtrippping Verilog?
code_blocks:
switch { a b }
  case c, d
    ...
end
